elements.sent-2411-108: Network Intrusion Detection (NID) remains a key area of research within the information security community, while also being relevant to Machine Learning (ML) practitioners.
elements.sent-2411-109: The latter generally aim to detect attacks using network features, which have been extracted from raw network data typically using dimensionality reduction methods, such as principal component analysis (PCA).
elements.sent-2411-110: However, PCA is not able to assess the relevance of features for the task at hand.
elements.sent-2411-111: Consequently, the features available are of varying quality, with some being entirely non-informative.
elements.sent-2411-112: From this, two major drawbacks arise.
elements.sent-2411-113: Firstly, trained and deployed models have to process large amounts of unnecessary data, therefore draining potentially costly resources.
elements.sent-2411-114: Secondly, the noise caused by the presence of irrelevant features can, in some cases, impede a model's ability to detect an attack.
elements.sent-2411-115: In order to deal with these challenges, we present Feature Selection for Network Intrusion Detection (FSNID) a novel information-theoretic method that facilitates the exclusion of non-informative features when detecting network intrusions.
elements.sent-2411-116: The proposed method is based on function approximation using a neural network, which enables a version of our approach that incorporates a recurrent layer.
elements.sent-2411-117: Consequently, this version uniquely enables the integration of temporal dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-118: Through an extensive set of experiments, we demonstrate that the proposed method selects a significantly reduced feature set, while maintaining NID performance.
elements.sent-2411-119: Code will be made available upon publication.
elements.sent-2411-120: Network Intrusion Detection (NID) remains a key focus of the information security community given its substantial economic impact.
elements.sent-2411-121: For example, IBM estimates showed that, in 2023, the average cost of a data breach for the afflicted party was USD 4.45 million \cite{ibmreport2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-122: During standard network operations malicious attacks are typically absent.
elements.sent-2411-123: Therefore, the earliest Intrusion Prevention Systems (IPSs) were statistical methods developed to detect irregularities in network data \cite{denning87 .}
elements.sent-2411-124: These methods were among the early implementations of anomaly detectors, a broader category of techniques that continues to be effective in NID \cite{lazarevic2003,jabez15 .}
elements.sent-2411-125: Despite their success \cite{ahmed2016 , traditional anomaly detection methods, primarily focused on single time series analysis, fall short in leveraging the interrelations among multiple data series.}
elements.sent-2411-126: In contrast, anomaly detection methods designed to exploit these inter-series relationships often face significant computational complexity challenges \cite{dobigeon07,harle14 .}
elements.sent-2411-127: Starting from these considerations, NID has also been studied as a tabular data classification problem \cite{kevric2017,javaid2016,subba2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-128: According to this paradigm, a machine learning (ML) model receives as input a vector that summarizes the network data at a given instance in time, and outputs a boolean or probability indicating whether or not the system is under attack, and, in some cases, what type of attack it is under.
elements.sent-2411-129: Usually, the vector of features is extracted from raw network data, which are collected and stored as packet capture (PCap) files.
elements.sent-2411-130: These can be represented as a highly-dimensional set of time-series.
elements.sent-2411-131: A dimensionality-reduction algorithm is then applied to extract individual features.
elements.sent-2411-132: Examples of techniques employed for this task include principal component analysis (PCA) \cite{pearson1901,purnama20 , Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) \cite{lachenbruch1979,chen23 and, more recently, deep learning-based approaches \cite{sun2020 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-133: Although these methods have the ability to cluster the data into features, they are unable to assess how relevant and informative they will be for classification.
elements.sent-2411-134: Consequently, some will be entirely non-informative and should be removed.
elements.sent-2411-135: Additionally, given the NID context, it is possible to outline three further reasons as to why one may wish to reduce the required feature data.
elements.sent-2411-136: Firstly, the acquisition of the network data may be costly since specific software (and, in some cases, hardware) has to be deployed.
elements.sent-2411-137: Secondly, measurements might introduce delays into the network operations.
elements.sent-2411-138: Finally, in the context of cybersecurity, minimizing measurements is of strategic importance.
elements.sent-2411-139: Since in certain situations, an advantage can be gained by giving the impression that the network is not being monitored at all.
elements.sent-2411-140: This has led to a growing body of work in feature selection for NID \cite{khammassi17,moustafa17,selvakumar19,khammassi20 .}
elements.sent-2411-141: It is possible to identify two groups of approaches.
elements.sent-2411-142: The first aims to maximize mutual information (MI), i.e., the correlation, between the chosen feature set and the attack vector labels \cite{peng05,gao16,brown12,chen18 .}
elements.sent-2411-143: However, this requires the definition of the desired set size.
elements.sent-2411-144: In feature-rich datasets, including those reporting network traffic, the search space of this hyperparameter becomes impractically large.
elements.sent-2411-145: Moreover, the complexity of these algorithms scales polynomially in time with the number of features to be assessed.
elements.sent-2411-146: Again, this is non-desirable for highly dimensional data.
elements.sent-2411-147: The second, and most common, general paradigm is to rank features based on MI \cite{LIU2009 , or Shapley values \cite{Shapley1953 , before selecting a predetermined number of the best performing features.}}
elements.sent-2411-148: However, these methods struggle to deal with highly correlated features \cite{Frye2020,Kumar2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-149: This issue is especially pertinent in network contexts, where homogeneous traffic patterns are repeated in individual features.
elements.sent-2411-150: Additionally, none of the methods discussed so far account for temporal dependencies, which are of key importance in NID.
elements.sent-2411-151: While the use of ML models with recurrent layers has been shown to enhance NID performance, the feature selection methods used to determine inputs for these models fail to leverage the same temporal relationships.
elements.sent-2411-152: Consequently, the set of selected features may lack crucial information that could otherwise be utilized effectively by the classification system.
elements.sent-2411-153: We identify four major challenges when selecting features for NID:.
elements.sent-2411-155: How does the complexity of the method scale with the number of selected features? (In NID where the number of features is large, complex algorithms quickly become impractical.).
elements.sent-2411-157: How does the feature selection method define the final number of selected features? (This is of particular relevance for datasets that have large numbers of features as the size of the resulting search space scales proportionally.).
elements.sent-2411-159: How does the feature selection method handle highly correlated variables? (This is key during NID as multiple features portrey similar traffic patterns.).
elements.sent-2411-161: Can the feature selection method incorporate temporal dependencies? (In NID incorporating temporal dependencies into classification tasks is known to improve accuracy, therefore, this should also inform the features selected.).
elements.sent-2411-162: In response to these challenges, we present Feature Selection for Network Intrusion Detection (FSNID), a novel information-theoretic method that naturally overcomes such problems.
elements.sent-2411-163: FSNID relies upon the ability to neurally approximate the entropy transferred from the labels dataset to each feature.
elements.sent-2411-164: Once this quantity is obtained, we then exclude variables from our set of desired features if they are deemed to be uninformative.
elements.sent-2411-165: Furthermore, given it relies upon function approximation, we can use recurrent layers to incorporate time-based dependencies within our Transfer Entropy (TE) calculation.
elements.sent-2411-166: For example, using a simple DNN to estimate the entropy transferred will lead to a value that only reflects the relationships present in the data at each iteration of training.
elements.sent-2411-167: Meanwhile, if we were to use an Long-Short Term Memory (LSTM) network \cite{hochreiter1997 to approximate this function we can also include relationships that span multiple iterations, overcoming the temporal relationships problem presented above.}
elements.sent-2411-168: We show that incorporating such dependencies reduces the number of required features further.
elements.sent-2411-169: Consequently, our method reduces the feature space significantly, while not degrading classification performance.
elements.sent-2411-170: The contributions of the paper can be summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-171: We develop a method of feature selection for NID, based on excluding variables that transfer negligible entropy to the attack vector labels.
elements.sent-2411-172: The computation of the TE-based measure in FSNID depends on general function approximation methods.
elements.sent-2411-173: By incorporating recurrent layers into this estimation process, we leverage temporal dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-174: We show experimentally that using the described techniques leads to significant reductions in the size of the feature space without degrading classification performance.
elements.sent-2411-175: Feature selection can be defined as the process of reducing the dimensionality of the input to the ML model while maintaining the same classification or regression performance.
elements.sent-2411-176: Methods for feature selection can be divided into two conceptual groups, namely wrapper and filter methods, where the latter have been deployed more commonly in the field of feature selection for NID due to their performance for high-dimensional feature spaces.
elements.sent-2411-178: These involve selecting variables due to the underlying relationships within the data, independently from the model itself.
elements.sent-2411-179: A popular method relies on Shapley values as a score for `feature importance' \cite{covert2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-180: Shapley values, originated by Shapley as a means to efficiently allocate resources in cooperative game theory \cite{Shapley1953 , were adapted for feature importance by having the input features `cooperate' to predict the output of an ML model \cite{Lunberg2017 .}}
elements.sent-2411-181: Despite Shapley value's widespread adoption, they fail to overcome the redundancy problem.
elements.sent-2411-182: Two perfectly correlated features, will both return Shapley values of zero, in spite of their importance for predicting the output of the model.
elements.sent-2411-183: Furthermore, \cite{janzing20 and \cite{Sundararajan20 showed that, in some cases, non-negative Shapley values could be assigned to features that have no impact on the final outcome of an ML model.}}
elements.sent-2411-184: In response to this, literature has been developed which overcomes such issues.
elements.sent-2411-185: Namely, the authors of \cite{janssen2023 and \cite{catav21 developed methods to assign feature importance's that adhere to certain properties, which have been pre-specified in their axioms for feature importance.}}
elements.sent-2411-186: One such axiom ensures that when calculating the second feature importance of a pair of perfectly correlated features, it must receive the same score as the first.
elements.sent-2411-187: This leads to scores that reflect the importance of a feature in predicting the model output, therefore, negating the redundancy issue as described by \cite{Kumar2020,Frye2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-188: However, the use of these scores lead to the following problem: when selecting features for a model, either of the correlated features is just as likely to be selected.
elements.sent-2411-190: In practice, we require only one of these features.
elements.sent-2411-191: In computer networks, and networks more generally, we often see highly correlated features.
elements.sent-2411-192: Consequently, Shapley-based methods for feature selection are not well suited to network traffic data.. Information-theoretic filter methods.
elements.sent-2411-193: As a result, information-theoretic filter methods are more commonly used for NID.
elements.sent-2411-194: These methods aim to exploit underlying relationships in the data to select the features without knowledge of the model in use.
elements.sent-2411-195: A standard method \cite{battiti94 (see also the variations presented in \cite{peng05,brown12 ) involves augmenting the feature that maximizes the MI between the chosen subset and the ground truth.}}
elements.sent-2411-196: However, such methods fail to overcome the length $k$ problem and the complexity problem.
elements.sent-2411-197: Another related method involves exploring all the permutations of the entire feature set to optimize the MI between the subset and the target variable \cite{gao16,chen18,yamada20 .}
elements.sent-2411-198: To manage the computationally intensive task of searching through permutations, such techniques limit the size of the search space by fixing the cardinality of these subsets as \( k \).
elements.sent-2411-199: However, the selected features cannot guarantee optimality, unless all possible values of $k$ are compared.
elements.sent-2411-200: Therefore, these methods also suffer from the length $k$ problem.
elements.sent-2411-201: Despite these drawbacks, both techniques represent the state of the art and they are widely adopted by the NID community \cite{amiri11,bostani17,selvakumar19 .}
elements.sent-2411-202: Feature selection methods for network intrusion detection.
elements.sent-2411-203: Many specialized feature selection methods for NID have been developed \cite{amiri11,bostani17,khammassi17,moustafa17,selvakumar19,khammassi20 .}
elements.sent-2411-204: Often, they ensure that the complexity of the algorithm scales non-polynomially in time with respect to the number of features, but this comes at the cost of robustness.
elements.sent-2411-205: For example, in \cite{chou2008 `irrelevant' features are removed on a case-by-case basis if they are uncorrelated with the target.}

elements.sent-2411-329: In this section, we outline the motivation and results of our experiments.
elements.sent-2411-330: Initially, we specify the research questions we aim to address in the evaluation of the method and we detail how these informed the selection of our comparative baselines.

elements.sent-2411-333: We now outline the specific research questions that are of importance when aiming to select optimal features for NID.

elements.sent-2411-336: Is FSNID scalable in the presence of large feature spaces?.
elements.sent-2411-337: In this section, we introduce five methods for feature selection that we will use as comparative baselines.
elements.sent-2411-338: As examples of classic lightweight and linear in time methods with respect to the number of features, we selected Permutation Importance (PI) and least absolute shrinkage and selection operator (LASSO) \cite{tibshirani1996regression,breiman2001 .}
elements.sent-2411-339: They are both highly scalable, and appropriate for the large datasets of interest.
elements.sent-2411-340: We also adopted the ultra marginal feature importance (UMFI) algorithm with optimal transport \cite{janssen2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-341: We chose this technique as it is a state-of-the-art method for assigning feature importance.
elements.sent-2411-342: To the best of our knowledge, this is the only technique that attempts to natively deal with highly correlated variables, in a manner that is linear in time with respect to the number of features.
elements.sent-2411-343: This is essential in the presence of highly dimensional feature spaces.
elements.sent-2411-344: We also compare to the mutual information firefly algorithm (MIFA) \cite{selvakumar19 , as a representative and popular example of metaheuristics.}
elements.sent-2411-345: Additionally, we include the classic and widely adopted Conditional Likelihood Maximization (CLM) framework by Brown et al. \cite{brown12 .}
elements.sent-2411-346: Unlike the first three baselines, the temporal complexity of MIFA and CLM is non-linear in time with respect to the number of features.
elements.sent-2411-347: Where possible, we utilize null models to determine whether or not a feature is informative.

elements.sent-2411-350: We first address RQ1 and RQ2 by presenting the results of an extensive evaluation, comparing the feature selection performance of FSNID and the baselines.

elements.sent-2411-355: For this study, we use datasets that explore a range of network and attck types.
elements.sent-2411-356: The datasets used in this study provide a comprehensive view of network traffic and differ significantly in their attributes and focus.
elements.sent-2411-357: The TON-IoT dataset offers diverse IoT and IIoT sensor data combined with system traces from Linux and Windows hosts, capturing various system activities and featuring multiple attack types and benign data \cite{moustafa2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-358: The NSL-KDD dataset, addressing the shortcomings of the KDD'99 dataset, includes numeric, binary, and nominal features, categorizing attacks into DoS, R2L, U2R, and Probe, with a variety of attack styles applicable to non-IoT networks \cite{tavallaee2009,selvakumar19 .}
elements.sent-2411-359: The CIC-DDoS2019 dataset focuses on DDoS attacks and real-world traffic conditions, particularly highlighting the unique UDPLag attacks \cite{sharafaldin2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-360: The UNSW-NB 15 dataset, consisting of multiple features and attack groups, requires consideration of time dependencies for high classification accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-361: Finally, the CICIDS17 dataset captures benign traffic and state-of-the-art attacks across various network flow features, ensuring real-world conditions are mirrored \cite{sharafaldin2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-362: These datasets essentially encompass a broad spectrum of attacks, each varying in frequency and occurring across different applications, systems, or devices.

elements.sent-2411-430: Feature selection is a fundamental problem in network intrusion detection.
elements.sent-2411-431: This is due to the computational cost associated to large input spaces and the requirement of minimizing monitoring for both economic and strategic reasons.
elements.sent-2411-432: In this paper, we have introduced FSNID, a new information-theoretic framework designed to select features for detecting network intrusions.
elements.sent-2411-433: Our approach estimates a TE-based `feature importance' using a neural function approximator.
elements.sent-2411-434: FSNID effectively identifies the minimal number of features even if they are highly correlated, while its computation time scales linearly relative to the number of features.
elements.sent-2411-435: All of these are desirable attributes when applied to highly-dimensional network data.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{FSNID.} \label{alg:algorithm} Input: Network features $\mathcal{X}$ and ground truth labels $Y$, respectively (see Section \ref{sec:preliminaries}). Output: ${\mathcal{X}}_*$ (a desirable subset of features). \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE Initialize ${\mathcal{X}}_* = \{\}$ \FOR{$i = 1$ to $N$} \IF{$\Phi_{{X}_i; {\mathcal{X}} \rightarrow {Y}} = 0$} \STATE ${\mathcal{X}} = {\mathcal{X}} \backslash \{{X}_i\}$ \ELSE \STATE ${\mathcal{X}}_* = {\mathcal{X}}_* \cup \{{X}_i\}$ \ENDIF \ENDFOR \STATE return ${\mathcal{X}}_*$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-507: Feature addition typically stops when the MI stops rising.
